perturbation.original_text: Mentor shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of, and avoid any unauthorised disclosure or use of Confidential Information. Such measures shall include the highest degree of care that Mentor utilises to protect Mentor ’s own confidential information of a similar nature, but no less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Mentor shall endeavor to take measures to protect the secrecy of, and avoid unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information. Such measures will include a degree of care Mentor subjectively believes is appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'all reasonable measures' with 'endeavor to take measures' and replaces 'highest degree of care that Mentor utilizes to protect Mentor's own confidential information of a similar nature, but no less than reasonable care' with 'degree of care Mentor subjectively believes is appropriate', making the obligation vague and subjective. This weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text mandates 'reasonable measures' and 'no less than reasonable care', aligning with the Trade Secrets Act's requirement for active protection. The modified text allows Mentor to use a 'degree of care Mentor subjectively believes is appropriate', which may not be sufficient to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, potentially violating the act.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1832 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1832 : Theft of trade secrets Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §1832. Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates 'reasonable measures' and 'no less than reasonable care' to protect confidential information. The changed text replaces this with a subjective standard ('degree of care Mentor subjectively believes is appropriate'), which may not meet the legal threshold for protecting trade secrets. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996 and 18 U.S.C. § 1832, which address theft of trade secrets, imply a standard of care that goes beyond subjective belief. While the snippets provided don't explicitly define 'reasonable care', they establish a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. By allowing the Mentor to decide what is 'appropriate' protection subjectively, the changed text creates a contradiction, undermining the need to actively prevent misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Payment shall be made within 30 days from the receipt of invoice, and following the invoice verification by Organiser. No deductions w ill be made from the gross fees paid to mentor who shall be solely responsible for ensuring that all and any Government taxes and other deductions are paid by him/her.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment shall be made within a reasonable time from the receipt of invoice, subject to invoice verification by Organiser. Deductions may be made from the gross fees paid to mentor as deemed necessary by the Organiser; the Mentor remains responsible for paying all taxes.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing '30 days' with 'a reasonable time' introduces ambiguity regarding the payment timeframe. Adding 'Deductions may be made from the gross fees paid to mentor as deemed necessary by the Organiser' creates uncertainty about the amount the Mentor will receive. This is problematic because it potentially contradicts labor laws related to prompt payment and deductions.
perturbation.law_explanation: German Civil Code (BGB) Section 286 concerns default of the debtor. Replacing '30 days' with 'reasonable time' is ambiguous and undermines the clarity required for payment terms under German law. Further, allowing arbitrary deductions contradicts the principle of agreed-upon compensation, creating a potential breach of contract if the 'reasonable time' is excessively long or deductions are unjustified.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 286 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 286 Verzug des Schuldners *) (1) Leistet der Schuldner auf eine Mahnung des Gläubigers nicht, die nach dem Eintritt der Fälligkeit erfolgt, so kommt er durch die Mahnung in Verzug. Der Mahnung stehen die Erhebung der Klage auf die Leistung sowie die Zustellung eines Mahnbescheids im Mahnverfahren gleich. (2) Der Mahnung bedarf es nicht, wenn 1. für die Leistung eine Zeit nach dem Kalender bestimmt ist, 2. der Leistung ein Ereignis vorauszugehen hat und eine angemessene Zeit für die Leistung in der Weise bestimmt ist, dass sie sich von dem Ereignis an nach dem Kalender berechnen lässt, 3. der Schuldner die Leistung ernsthaft und endgültig verweigert, 4. aus besonderen Gründen unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen der sofortige Eintritt des Verzugs gerechtfertigt ist. (3) Der Schuldner einer Entgeltforderung kommt spätestens in Verzug, wenn er nicht innerhalb von 30 Tagen nach Fälligkeit und Zugang einer Rechnung oder gleichwertigen Zahlungsaufstellung leistet; dies gilt gegenüber einem Schuldner, der Verbraucher ist, nur, wenn auf diese Folgen in der Rechnung oder Zahlungsaufstellung besonders hingewiesen worden ist. Wenn der Zeitpunkt des Zugangs der Rechnung oder Zahlungsaufstellung unsicher ist, kommt der Schuldner, der nicht Verbraucher ist, spätestens 30 Tage nach Fälligkeit und Empfang der Gegenleistung in Verzug. (4) Der Schuldner kommt nicht in Verzug, solange die Leistung infolge eines Umstands unterbleibt, den er nicht zu vertreten hat. (5) Für eine von den Absätzen 1 bis 3 abweichende Vereinbarung über den Eintritt des Verzugs gilt § 271a Absatz 1 bis 5 entsprechend. *) Amtlicher Hinweis: Diese Vorschrift dient zum Teil auch der Umsetzung der Richtlinie 2000/35/EG des Europäischen Parlaments und des Rates vom 29. Juni 2000 zur Bekämpfung von Zahlungsverzug im Geschäftsverkehr (ABl. EG Nr. L 200 S. 35). Fußnote (+++ § 286: Zur Anwendung vgl. § 34 BGBEG +

(2) Der Mahnung bedarf es nicht, wenn 1. für die Leistung eine Zeit nach dem Kalender bestimmt ist, 2. der Leistung ein Ereignis vorauszugehen hat und eine angemessene Zeit für die Leistung in der Weise bestimmt ist, dass sie sich von dem Ereignis an nach de
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding payment timeframe and allows for arbitrary deductions, potentially contradicting German law (BGB § 286) concerning debtor's default and agreed-upon compensation. The original text specified '30 days,' whereas the changed text uses 'a reasonable time,' weakening the clarity required for payment terms. Snippet 1 of § 286 BGB states that the debtor is in default '...wenn er nicht innerhalb von 30 Tagen nach Fälligkeit und Zugang einer Rechnung...leistet.' The changed text creates uncertainty and could lead to payment delays beyond the legally accepted timeframe, which undermines the legal obligation for prompt payment. Additionally, the clause 'Deductions may be made from the gross fees paid to mentor as deemed necessary by the Organiser' contradicts the principle of agreed-upon compensation, creating a potential breach of contract.

perturbation.original_text: Monthly v irtual interactions need to be planned by Mentor, to ensure a continuous acceleration of the Participants Ideas ´ towards business maturity.
perturbation.changed_text: Mentor should consider occasional virtual interactions, with the goal of potentially contributing to the Participants' Ideas' progress towards business maturity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a requirement for 'monthly virtual interactions', while the modified text only suggests that a Mentor 'should consider occasional virtual interactions'. Replacing 'need to be planned' with 'should consider' and replacing 'ensure a continuous acceleration' with 'potentially contributing' weakens the commitment and the required frequency of contact. This makes the contractual requirement unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract principles under German Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under German Law, Section 280 BGB addresses damages for breach of contract. By weakening the obligation to have 'monthly virtual interactions', the altered text provides no grounds to find a breach of contract as no precise action can be legally enforced based on an ambiguous term. The original text contained a clear action (monthly meetings) for which a breach could be determined based on failure to act. The new modified term now fails this test.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 280 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 280 Schadensersatz wegen Pflichtverletzung (1) Verletzt der Schuldner eine Pflicht aus dem Schuldverhältnis, so kann der Gläubiger Ersatz des hierdurch entstehenden Schadens verlangen. Dies gilt nicht, wenn der Schuldner die Pflichtverletzung nicht zu vertreten hat. (2) Schadensersatz wegen Verzögerung der Leistung kann der Gläubiger nur unter der zusätzlichen Voraussetzung des § 286 verlangen. (3) Schadensersatz statt der Leistung kann der Gläubiger nur unter den zusätzlichen Voraussetzungen des § 281, des § 282 oder des § 283 verlangen. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the contractual obligation by changing 'monthly virtual interactions need to be planned' to 'Mentor should consider occasional virtual interactions'. The Law Explanation points out that under German Law, Section 280 BGB addresses damages for breach of contract. The original text contained a clear action for which a breach could be determined based on failure to act (monthly meetings). The modified term makes it harder to prove a breach of contract, since occasional is ambiguous and the shift from 'need to be planned' to 'should consider' reduces the legal obligation on the mentor to perform any specific action. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references § 280 BGB, which supports the Law Explanation's reasoning.

perturbation.original_text: Mentor confirms that Mentor has not been involved in the development of any of the Ideas to be coached at any stage and th at Mentor has no personal or commercial connection to any Participant of the Copernicus Accelerator that would influence an objective development of Ideas.
perturbation.changed_text: Mentor generally believes that Mentor has had limited involvement in the development of the Ideas to be coached and Mentor is not consciously aware of any significant personal or commercial connection to any Participant that would substantially inhibit the objective development of Ideas.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear declaration of no prior involvement or conflict of interest. The modified text introduces subjective and vague qualifiers such as 'generally believes,' 'limited involvement,' 'not consciously aware,' and 'substantially inhibit,' which weaken the assurance of impartiality and objectivity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Good Faith under German Law
perturbation.law_explanation: German Civil Code Section 242 BGB requires parties to act in good faith. The original clause ensures transparency and prevents conflicts of interest. The modified clause, with its subjective qualifications, allows for potential undisclosed conflicts and compromises the good faith requirement by making it difficult to prove a violation, since 'limited involvement', 'not consciously aware', and 'substantially inhibit' are ambiguous and leave room for interpretation that may not align with the principle of good faith in contractual relationships.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that undermines the original intent of ensuring no conflict of interest, potentially violating the good faith requirement under German law. The original contract language explicitly stated 'no personal or commercial connection,' while the modified version weakens this with subjective phrases like 'generally believes,' 'limited involvement,' and 'not consciously aware,' opening the door for potential undisclosed conflicts. This shift contradicts the principle of acting in good faith (§ 242 BGB), as the vagueness makes it difficult to prove a violation. The snippets confirm that debtors must perform obligations in good faith.
